title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Dr. Penrose informs Gwendolyn Reed that her father, Colonel Kelvin Hardy, committed suicide by overdosing on a secret stockpile of sleeping pills. The doctor clarifies that while Hardy suffered from delusions—including the belief that his wife, Helen, was poisoning him—he was not a murderer. Dr. Penrose suggests that Hardy's conviction that he had killed Helen stemmed from a psychological desire for punishment after discovering a note stating she was leaving him. To assist Gwendolyn, Dr. Penrose provides her with an old black notebook containing Hardy's case notes, which had been preserved by a Dr. McQuail.

While traveling back to London by train, Gwendolyn reads the notebook. The entries reveal Hardy's mental instability and his suspicion that Helen was hiding something or had a lover. He records a memory of finding a note in a dining room followed by "darkness," a sequence that led him to believe he had strangled her. Hardy also expresses a desire to end his life to prevent a scandal and to protect Gweneth, whom he mentions is in New Zealand. As Gwendolyn reads, moved to tears, she notices a headline in a passenger's newspaper asking, "Who is the man in her life?", which mirrors the suspicions recorded in the diary.

Meanwhile, Miss Marple walks through a seafront square and enters an embroidery shop on Fore Street. While purchasing turquoise yarn, she engages in polite conversation with the shop assistants, noting that she has not visited the area in nineteen years.
